summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a stem cell?

Stem cells have two defining properties: (1) self-renewal — the ability to divide and produce more stem cells, maintaining the stem cell pool, and (2) potency — the ability to differentiate into one or more specialized cell types. These properties make them fundamentally different from most body cells, which are terminally differentiated and perform specific functions but cannot produce new cell types.

What is the difference between totipotent, pluripotent, and multipotent stem cells?

Stem cell potency decreases as development progresses. Totipotent cells (the zygote and first few divisions) can form an entire organism including extraembryonic tissues (placenta). Pluripotent cells (inner cell mass of blastocyst → embryonic stem cells) can become any of the ~200+ cell types in the body but not placenta. Multipotent cells (adult stem cells, like hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow) can only differentiate into a limited range of related cell types within their tissue lineage.

What is cellular differentiation?

Differentiation is the developmental process where a cell progressively specializes — activating certain genes and silencing others — to acquire the specific structure and function of a mature cell type. All cells in an organism contain the same DNA, but different cell types express different subsets of genes. For example, a stem cell might differentiate into a muscle cell (expressing actin, myosin) or a neuron (expressing ion channels, neurotransmitter receptors). Differentiation is usually irreversible under normal conditions, though iPSC technology reverses it artificially.

What is regenerative medicine?

Regenerative medicine is an interdisciplinary field that combines stem cell biology, tissue engineering, biomaterials science, and molecular biology to develop therapies that repair, replace, or regenerate damaged or diseased tissues and organs. Approaches include: stem cell transplantation (injecting cells to repair tissue), tissue engineering (growing tissues on biodegradable scaffolds), 3D bioprinting (layer-by-layer construction of tissue), and gene therapy (correcting genetic defects in a patient's own cells). The ultimate goal is to restore normal tissue function.

What is somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT), and what was its most famous application?

Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) involves removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with the nucleus from an adult somatic cell. The reconstructed egg is stimulated to begin dividing, creating an embryo that is a genetic clone of the nuclear donor. In 1996, Ian Wilmut used SCNT to create Dolly the sheep from a mammary gland cell — proving that differentiated adult cells retain all genetic information needed to produce a complete organism. SCNT is used in research to produce patient-matched ESCs (therapeutic cloning) without requiring fertilization.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the difference between totipotent, pluripotent, and multipotent stem cells?

Stem cell potency decreases as development progresses. Totipotent cells (the zygote and first few divisions) can form an entire organism including extraembryonic tissues (placenta). Pluripotent cells (inner cell mass of blastocyst → embryonic stem cells) can become any of the ~200+ cell types in the body but not placenta. Multipotent cells (adult stem cells, like hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow) can only differentiate into a limited range of related cell types within their tissue lineage.

Example: Which definition BEST describes 'Totipotent'?

Totipotent (Latin: totus = whole, potens = powerful) describes cells with the broadest developmental potential — they can give rise to every cell type needed for a complete organism, including all embryonic cell types AND extraembryonic structures like the placenta, umbilical cord, and amniotic membranes. Only the zygote and cells from the first ~3-4 cell divisions (up to about the 16-cell morula stage) are truly totipotent. After that, cells begin to specialize and lose totipotency, becoming pluripotent in the inner cell mass of the blastocyst.

Example: Which definition BEST describes 'Multipotent'?

Multipotent (Latin: multus = many) describes stem cells with more restricted potency than pluripotent cells — they can produce multiple cell types but only within a related lineage. Most adult stem cells are multipotent. For example, hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) in bone marrow can differentiate into red blood cells, white blood cells (neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes), and platelets — all blood cell types — but cannot become neurons, muscle cells, or skin cells. Neural stem cells can produce neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes but not blood cells.

Example: Dolly the sheep, cloned via SCNT in 1996, developed arthritis and lung disease and was euthanized at age 6 (sheep typically live ~12 years). Some scientists speculated her premature aging was linked to the fact that the donor nucleus came from a 6-year-old ewe's mammary cell. What cellular factor could explain premature aging in Dolly?

Telomere length is a biological clock — telomeres (protective DNA caps on chromosome ends) shorten with each cell division, and when they become critically short, cells enter senescence or die. The mammary cell used to clone Dolly had already undergone 6 years of divisions, shortening its telomeres. While the egg cytoplasm can partially reset epigenetic marks (reprogramming), telomere restoration by telomerase may have been incomplete. This means Dolly's cells may have started life with the biological age of a 6-year-old sheep. This 'telomere problem' is one of several reprogramming challenges in SCNT cloning (though later studies on Dolly's cloned 'sisters' showed healthier aging).

Example: A clinical trial uses iPSC-derived retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells to treat age-related macular degeneration (AMD), a leading cause of blindness in the elderly. The RPE cells are transplanted beneath the patient's retina. Why is the eye considered an ideal early target for stem cell therapy compared to other organs?

The eye has several features that make it an ideal 'proving ground' for stem cell therapies: (1) Immune privilege — the blood-retinal barrier and local immunosuppressive factors limit immune cell access, reducing rejection risk. (2) Small cell requirement — AMD involves loss of RPE, a single-cell-thick layer; only ~100,000-500,000 cells are needed per transplant. (3) Accessible anatomy — cells can be precisely delivered via subretinal injection. (4) Measurable outcomes — OCT imaging and visual acuity tests can objectively track results. (5) Built-in control — the untreated eye provides comparison. The RIKEN Center in Japan and Moorfields Eye Hospital in London have conducted pioneering iPSC-RPE trials.

Example: Scientists grow 'mini-brains' (cerebral organoids) in a dish by differentiating iPSCs through stages that recapitulate embryonic brain development. These organoids develop cortical layers, neurons, and glial cells. Researchers use them to study Zika virus infection during brain development. What advantage do organoids offer over traditional 2D cell cultures and animal models?

Organoids bridge the gap between oversimplified 2D cell cultures and complex (but not always human-relevant) animal models. When iPSCs are cultured in 3D with appropriate growth factors, they self-organize into structures resembling miniature organs — cerebral organoids develop layered cortical tissue, intestinal organoids form crypt-villus structures, and kidney organoids form nephron-like segments. For Zika research, cerebral organoids revealed that the virus preferentially infects human neural progenitor cells, causing microcephaly-like growth reduction — a finding that was difficult to replicate in mice. Organoids also enable personalized medicine: patient-specific organoids can test drug responses for that individual.
